claim: A "decay-by-outcome" field that records when a stored piece of logic proved wrong, surfaced at retrieval as a confidence level, prevents stale advice from resurfacing as gospel.

research.summary: The claim has two separable parts. (a) TERM: "decay-by-outcome" is NOT an established, named pattern. Targeted searches return zero primary sources using the phrase in this sense; the only hits are unrelated neuroscience ("lose-shift" memory traces in rats). So as a coined label it is not recognized jargon. (b) IDEA: The underlying mechanism — recording when stored logic proved wrong and surfacing that at retrieval as a (lowered) confidence so stale advice stops being treated as gospel — is technically sound and actively researched, but it is a COMPOSITE of several established, separately-named mechanisms rather than one named pattern. Critically, the claim's framing is most precise as a CONTRAST to the best-known memory-decay system: MemoryBank (Zhong et al., AAAI 2024) decays memory by TIME ELAPSED and IMPORTANCE via the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve (R=e^(-t/S)) — it does NOT decay by outcome/correctness. So "decay-by-outcome" is precisely the thing MemoryBank does NOT do; the claim correctly identifies a real gap. The closest PRIMARY research to the actual idea is the STALE benchmark + CUPMem (arXiv 2605.06527), which formalizes "implicit conflict" — a later observation rendering an earlier belief invalid under world knowledge without explicit negation — and adjudicates memories as KEEP/STALE/REPLACE/UNKNOWN. That is outcome/conflict-driven invalidation, but it uses discrete status labels, not a continuous decaying confidence level. Reflexion (Shinn et al., NeurIPS 2023) records WRONG outcomes (binary success/fail converted to verbal feedback) into long-term episodic memory and surfaces them on the next attempt — exactly the "remember when this was wrong, use it next time" loop — but the signal is purely verbal, with NO numeric confidence that decays. The "confidence that drops on contradiction" sub-mechanism is, formally, Bayesian belief updating / temporal credit assignment applied to a memory store; multiple agent-memory systems (e.g. confidence-weighted memory units updated via Bayesian update; half-life confidence applied at read time, flat until an invalidating event) implement pieces of it. CONCLUSION: not an established term; sound idea; best described as outcome-/conflict-based belief invalidation (STALE/CUPMem) + verbal-RL failure memory (Reflexion) + Bayesian confidence updating — and explicitly distinct from Ebbinghaus time-decay (MemoryBank).

evidence.source: MemoryBank: Enhancing Large Language Models with Long-Term Memory, Zhong et al., AAAI 2024 / arXiv:2305.10250 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10250)
evidence.what_it_says: Abstract verbatim: MemoryBank's updating mechanism is 'inspired by the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve theory, which permits the AI to forget and reinforce memory based on time elapsed and the relative significance of the memory.' Decay is driven by TIME and IMPORTANCE (exponential R=e^(-t/S)), NOT by whether stored info was proven wrong. This establishes that the canonical memory-decay system in this space is decay-by-time, so 'decay-by-outcome' names a different, complementary mechanism that MemoryBank does not provide.

evidence.source: STALE: Can LLM Agents Know When Their Memories Are No Longer Valid? arXiv:2605.06527 (https://arxiv.org/html/2605.06527v1)
evidence.what_it_says: Closest primary research to the IDEA. Formally defines 'implicit conflict': 'a new observation m_n renders a previously supported belief invalid under world knowledge K, without this invalidation being explicitly communicated' (axioms of belief incompatibility + non-explicit invalidation). The proposed CUPMem adjudicates memories at write time into KEEP / STALE / REPLACE / UNKNOWN. This is outcome/conflict-driven invalidation aimed at exactly the 'stale advice resurfacing' problem — but it uses discrete status labels, NOT a continuous decaying confidence score, and it does NOT use the term 'decay-by-outcome' or 'outcome-weighted'.

evidence.source: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning, Shinn et al., NeurIPS 2023 / arXiv:2303.11366 (https://arxiv.org/html/2303.11366)
evidence.what_it_says: Implements the 'record when logic proved wrong, surface it next time' loop: converts a sparse/binary success-fail reward into a verbal self-reflection stored in long-term episodic memory, then conditions the next trial on it. Directly supports the soundness of learning from wrong outcomes in memory. BUT feedback is 'entirely linguistic, without numeric confidence decays' — there is no decaying confidence LEVEL, which is the specific instrumentation the claim describes. Also notes the failure mode: agents can 'store incorrect lessons in persistent memory' (degeneration of thought), a caveat for the claim's reliability.

evidence.source: Targeted web searches for the exact phrase '"decay-by-outcome"' and '"outcome-based decay" memory belief confidence' (Bing/Google via WebSearch, June 2026)
evidence.what_it_says: No primary or secondary source uses 'decay-by-outcome' as a named software/AI memory pattern. The only literal-ish hit is unrelated neuroscience: a rat study on the 'memory trace supporting lose-shift responding' decaying after reward omission (PMC5112541). Confirms the TERM is coined/not established (is_established_term=false), even though the idea is real.

evidence.source: Bayesian / credit-assignment framing — e.g. 'Rewarding Beliefs, Not Actions: Consistency-Guided Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon Agents' (arXiv:2605.20061) and 'On Prior Confidence and Belief Updating' (arXiv:2412.10662)
evidence.what_it_says: The 'confidence drops when contradicted' sub-mechanism is formally Bayesian belief updating + temporal credit assignment: people/agents 'update more when less confident,' and overconfidence in a belief causes agents to 'continue selecting actions consistent with a mistaken assumption even after contradictory observations' — precisely the stale-gospel failure the claim targets. This grounds the idea in established RL/Bayesian theory rather than a novel pattern.

evidence.source: Practitioner/secondary corroboration of confidence-on-contradiction in agent memory: confidence-weighted memory units updated via Bayesian update (Dynamic Affective Memory Management, arXiv:2510.27418) and half-life confidence applied at read time / flat until an invalidating event (Springdrift, arXiv:2604.04660; 'When Agent Memory Learns to Forget', Medium)
evidence.what_it_says: Multiple recent systems implement pieces of the mechanism: confidence as a probability distribution updated by new evidence (strong contradiction reduces confidence and opinion content); confidence that 'stays flat until an external event invalidates it — a user contradiction, system event, or new conflicting fact'; and confidence surfaced at retrieval to stop stale facts dominating. Shows the idea is buildable and being built, but as scattered, differently-named features — not under a single 'decay-by-outcome' label.

I tried to kill this and could not kill the IDEA; the NAMED pattern does die, which matches the researcher's "mixed / is_established_term=false / idea_soundness=sound" leaning. Verdict: the researcher's overall leaning SURVIVES (refuted=false), because the load-bearing assertions check out against primary sources.

NAMED PATTERN — FALSE (high bar, fails). My own WebSearch for the literal "decay-by-outcome" returned zero hits using it as a named AI/software memory pattern; the only "outcome-based decay" hits in the literature mean something different (outcome-level RL reward for training memory policies, e.g. RL4LRM/MemAgent/MEM1), not "record when stored logic proved wrong and decay its confidence." So the term is coined jargon, not established. Researcher correctly flagged this.

IDEA — SOUND (lower bar, passes), and I verified the flagship primary source rather than trusting the summary. STALE (arXiv:2605.06527) is real and accurately described: it formalizes "implicit conflict" (a new observation invalidates a prior belief without explicit negation), shows frontier models fail at it (best ~55.2% accuracy), and its CUPMem prototype does write-time adjudication (KEEP/STALE/REPLACE), lifting GPT-4o-mini from 8.7% to 68.0%. This is outcome/conflict-driven invalidation aimed at exactly the stale-advice-as-gospel problem the claim names. Reflexion (arXiv:2303.11366, NeurIPS 2023) confirms the "record when logic proved wrong, condition next attempt on it" loop. Springdrift (arXiv:2604.04660) confirms confidence surfaced at READ time so stale facts lose influence.

THREE HONEST CAVEATS that keep this at "mixed," not a clean win for the claim as literally worded: (1) Every real system implements a PIECE, under a different name, and the dominant instrumentations are NOT the claim's "decaying confidence level driven by being-proven-wrong" — STALE/CUPMem uses discrete status labels (not a continuous score), and Springdrift's continuous decay is driven by TIME (half-life) with invalidation as a discrete reset event, not by outcome. MemoryBank's canonical decay is Ebbinghaus time+importance, explicitly not outcome. So the specific fusion the claim describes (continuous confidence that decays specifically because logic was proven wrong, surfaced at retrieval) is buildable and partially built, but is not demonstrated as a single coherent, validated mechanism in any one primary source. (2) Reflexion itself documents the failure mode that the claim glosses over: agents can "store incorrect lessons in persistent memory" — so the mechanism is not unconditionally reliable at "preventing" stale gospel; it can create new stale gospel. (3) The claim's strong verb "prevents" is overstated relative to evidence: STALE shows even best methods leave a large gap (acting on updated evidence is still failing ~32-45% of the time), so "reduces, when well-implemented" is supportable; "prevents" is not.

Independent WebSearch found no AI/software source using the phrase as a named pattern; the literature's "outcome-based decay" refers to RL outcome-reward training of memory policies, a different concept. IDEA: SOUND — the underlying mechanism (record that stored logic was contradicted, lower its standing, surface that at retrieval to stop stale advice resurfacing) is grounded in real primary work (STALE/CUPMem arXiv:2605.06527, Reflexion arXiv:2303.11366) and is being built (Springdrift). But it lives as scattered, differently-named features (discrete KEEP/STALE/REPLACE labels OR time-based half-life with invalidation resets), NOT as the specific continuous outcome-driven confidence decay the claim describes, and the claim's verb "prevents" overstates evidence that shows large residual failure rates plus a risk of storing incorrect lessons.

votes.reason: The claim is presented as a named pattern ("a 'decay-by-outcome' field"), and that named pattern is refuted. Independent verification confirms no primary or secondary source uses "decay-by-outcome" or "outcome-based decay." The WebSearch heading "Decay-by-Outcome Principle" was a search-engine synthesis, not a source quote — the actual closest article (Nexumo, "When Agent Memory Learns to Forget") uses entirely different vocabulary ("confidence-decay eviction," "contradiction-triggered eviction," "event-driven invalidation") and never the claimed term. Crucially, that article treats time-based confidence decay and contradiction-based invalidation as TWO SEPARATE mechanisms, which undercuts the claim that they form one coherent "decay-by-outcome" thing. The single closest primary research, STALE/CUPMem (arXiv:2605.06527, verified real), adjudicates memories into discrete labels {KEEP, STALE, REPLACE, UNKNOWN} at write time — NOT a continuous decaying "confidence level" surfaced at retrieval, which is the specific instrumentation the claim describes. The strong-form claim that this "prevents stale advice from resurfacing as gospel" is also overstated: Reflexion (NeurIPS 2023) documents the inverse failure mode where agents "store incorrect lessons in persistent memory" (degeneration of thought), so an outcome-tagging mechanism can itself ossify a wrong correction rather than prevent staleness.
votes.distinction: NAMED PATTERN: refuted (high confidence). "Decay-by-outcome" is coined jargon — zero sources use it; adjacent practitioner work names the pieces differently and splits the mechanism into separate time-decay vs. contradiction-invalidation features. IDEA: directionally sound but only in a weaker form than stated. Recording when stored logic proved wrong and dampening its retrieval authority is grounded in STALE/CUPMem, Reflexion, and Bayesian belief-updating. However, the specific instrumentation claimed — a continuous "confidence level" surfaced at retrieval driven by being-proven-wrong — is NOT how the leading research does it (STALE uses discrete write-time labels; Reflexion uses verbal-only feedback with no numeric confidence). And the "prevents stale advice as gospel" promise is contradicted by Reflexion's documented failure mode of persisting incorrect lessons. So: sound as a loose idea, refuted as a named pattern, and overclaimed in its strong "prevents" framing.

votes.reason:
Refuted AS A NAMED PATTERN; the underlying IDEA survives. Splitting the two questions as instructed:

NAMED PATTERN ("decay-by-outcome") — NOT established (high bar fails decisively). Two independent term searches (researcher's plus my own) return zero primary or secondary sources using "decay-by-outcome" or "outcome-based decay" as a named AI/software memory pattern. The only literal-adjacent hit is unrelated rat neuroscience. The term is coined jargon. This alone justifies refuted:true for the claim as phrased, because the claim presents "decay-by-outcome" as if it were a recognized mechanism with backing.

IDEA (record-when-wrong → surface-as-confidence → suppress stale advice) — SOUND but assembled, never shipped as the specific instrument described. I verified the three load-bearing primaries are real and say what's claimed: MemoryBank (arXiv:2305.10250, AAAI 2024) decays by TIME+IMPORTANCE via Ebbinghaus, explicitly NOT by outcome — so it does not support the claim, it contrasts with it. Reflexion (arXiv:2303.11366, NeurIPS 2023) records wrong outcomes into episodic memory and conditions next trial, but feedback is purely linguistic with NO decaying confidence level. STALE (arXiv:2605.06527, May 2026) is the closest match — its CUPMem adjudicates memories into KEEP/STALE/REPLACE/UNKNOWN to stop stale beliefs resurfacing — but uses DISCRETE status labels, not a continuous decaying confidence score, and does not use the claim's terminology.

Three problems weakening even the idea-soundness:
1. The claim conflates two distinct designs. Real systems either (a) flag staleness with discrete labels (STALE) or (b) apply continuous half-life decay by TIME (MemoryBank, Springdrift). NO verified primary source implements the exact thing claimed — a CONTINUOUS confidence that DECAYS specifically as a function of WRONG-OUTCOME EVENTS, surfaced at retrieval. It is stitched together from differently-named, differently-mechanized features.
2. STALE's own headline finding is a strong caveat the claim ignores: even the best frontier model hits only 55.2% accuracy at recognizing invalidated memories; agents "retrieve updated evidence but fail to act on it." So "prevents stale advice from resurfacing as gospel" is aspirational, not demonstrated — the mechanism the claim names is precisely where current systems FAIL.
3. Reflexion documents the inverse failure mode ("degeneration of thought" — agents store INCORRECT lessons in persistent memory). An outcome-keyed confidence field can itself be poisoned by mislabeled outcomes, so the claim's reliability is not established even in principle.

Bottom line: the claim overstates. As a named pattern it is fabricated/unestablished (refuted). As an idea it is buildable and grounded in Bayesian/credit-assignment theory, but the specific decaying-confidence-by-outcome instrument is not shown to exist or to work — the nearest real system reports majority failure at the exact task.
votes.distinction: NAMED PATTERN "decay-by-outcome": not established — zero primary/secondary sources use it; coined jargon (high bar clearly fails). IDEA: sound in principle (learning from wrong outcomes is grounded in Reflexion + Bayesian credit assignment), but the SPECIFIC instrument described (continuous confidence that decays by wrong-outcome events, surfaced at retrieval) is assembled from disparate features and not demonstrated end-to-end; the closest real system (STALE/CUPMem) uses discrete labels and reports only 55.2% best-model accuracy at this very task. So: FALSE as named pattern, sound-but-overstated as idea.
